name: Hakata Station
summary: Fukuoka's main railway hub connecting the city to the rest of Kyushu.
description: Hakata Station is Fukuoka's central transport hub and our arrival point on Day 1. The station complex houses shopping malls, restaurants, and easy access to the Fukuoka subway. Look out for the Hakata Dontaku decorations if visiting in the right season.

name: Hotel Forza Hakata
summary: Our base in Fukuoka — modern hotel a short walk from Hakata Station.
description: Hotel Forza Hakata is our home for the first nights in Fukuoka. It's a comfortable modern hotel within walking distance of Hakata Station, making it easy to drop off luggage and head straight out to explore. Breakfast is included.

name: Ichiran Ramen Honten
summary: The original Ichiran — solo-booth ramen at its finest, right in Fukuoka where it all began.
description: Ichiran started right here in Fukuoka, and the Honten (main store) is a pilgrimage for ramen lovers. You order on a form, sit in a solo booth, and the ramen arrives through a bamboo curtain. The tonkotsu broth is rich and creamy. Perfect for the first dinner in Japan.

name: Ohori Park
summary: A beautiful urban park built around a large central lake with a Japanese garden.
description: Ohori Park is one of Fukuoka's most loved green spaces, built around a large pond that was once a moat for Fukuoka Castle. The park features a central island connected by bridges, a traditional Japanese garden, and walking paths perfect for a morning stroll. In spring it fills with cherry blossom.

name: Fukuoka Castle Ruins
summary: Ruins of a grand 17th-century castle with sweeping views over Fukuoka city.
description: The ruins of Fukuoka Castle sit atop Maizuru Hill adjacent to Ohori Park. While the main keep no longer stands, several turrets and walls remain. Climb to the top for panoramic views across Fukuoka. The surrounding park is a popular picnic and hanami spot.

name: Kushida Shrine
summary: Fukuoka's most beloved shrine, home to the famous Hakata Gion Yamakasa festival float.
description: Kushida Shrine is the spiritual heart of Hakata. Founded in 757 AD, it enshrines the deity of the Hakata Gion Yamakasa festival. You can see one of the massive Yamakasa festival floats displayed year-round inside the shrine grounds. The giant ginkgo tree is said to be over 1000 years old.

name: Canal City Hakata
summary: A massive shopping and entertainment complex built around a winding canal.
description: Canal City Hakata is a city-within-a-city — a sprawling shopping mall built around an artificial canal running through the centre. With over 250 shops, restaurants, a cinema, and regular fountain shows, it is one of Fukuoka's most visited attractions. Great for last-minute Japanese snacks and souvenirs.

name: Yanagibashi Rengo Market
summary: Fukuoka's lively 'kitchen' market — fresh seafood, produce, and local street food.
description: Known as Fukuoka's kitchen, Yanagibashi Market is a covered arcade packed with fresh seafood vendors, greengrocers, and small eateries. Arrive early morning to see it at its most vibrant. Great for a cheap breakfast or picking up snacks. The sushi here is exceptional value.

name: Nakasu Yatai Food Stalls
summary: Fukuoka's iconic open-air yatai (food stalls) lining the Naka River — a must-do evening experience.
description: The yatai of Nakasu are a Fukuoka institution. These open-air stalls set up along the riverbank every evening serving tonkotsu ramen, yakitori, oden, and cold Asahi beer. The atmosphere is warm and lively with locals and tourists side by side. Plan to come hungry and stay for a few hours.

name: ANA Crowne Plaza Nagasaki
summary: Our Nagasaki hotel — well-located with great harbour views.
description: The ANA Crowne Plaza Nagasaki Gloverhill sits up on the hillside with views over the harbour. It is conveniently located near Glover Garden and the Oura Church. The hotel has excellent restaurants and an onsen-style bath.

name: Glover Garden
summary: Open-air museum of Western-style Meiji-era mansions with sweeping harbour views.
description: Glover Garden is an open-air museum on a hillside overlooking Nagasaki Harbour. It preserves the homes of foreign merchants who lived here during the Meiji period. The highlight is Glover House, Japan's oldest surviving Western-style wooden building. The harbour view from the top is spectacular.

name: Nagasaki Peace Memorial Park
summary: A solemn and moving memorial park marking the site of the 1945 atomic bomb hypocentre.
description: The Nagasaki Peace Memorial Park stands near the hypocentre of the 1945 atomic bomb explosion. The iconic Peace Statue by Seibo Kitamura dominates the northern section. The adjacent Atomic Bomb Museum provides a deeply moving account of the bombing and its aftermath. A profoundly important site to visit with respect.

name: Dejima Wharf
summary: Reconstructed Dutch trading post island — Nagasaki's window to the outside world during Japan's isolation era.
description: Dejima was an artificial island built in 1634 as the sole trading post allowed during Japan's sakoku (closed country) period. Dutch traders were confined here for over 200 years. The island has been faithfully reconstructed with period-accurate buildings, artefacts, and costumed guides. A fascinating slice of history.

name: Nagasaki Chinatown (Shinchi)
summary: Japan's oldest Chinatown — birthplace of Nagasaki Champon noodles.
description: Nagasaki Chinatown (Shinchi) is one of the oldest in Japan, dating to the 17th century when Chinese merchants settled here. The street is lined with restaurants serving Nagasaki's signature dishes: champon (thick noodles in a pork and seafood broth) and sara udon (crispy noodles). An essential stop for lunch.

name: Meganebashi Bridge
summary: Japan's oldest stone arch bridge — its reflection in the water creates the famous 'spectacles' shape.
description: Meganebashi (Spectacles Bridge) was built in 1634 by a Chinese Zen monk and is Japan's oldest stone arch bridge. Its two arches reflect in the Nakashima River to create the shape of a pair of spectacles. The riverside walk nearby is lovely and dotted with heart-shaped stones to find.

name: Mt. Inasa Observatory
summary: One of Japan's top three night views — a panoramic sweep of Nagasaki by night.
description: Mt. Inasa (332m) offers what is considered one of Japan's top three night views. A ropeway takes you to the summit where the observatory looks out over the entire bowl of Nagasaki and its harbour glittering at night. Time your visit for sunset to watch the city light up as darkness falls.

name: JR Kagoshima-Chuo Station
summary: Kagoshima's main station and southern terminus of the Kyushu Shinkansen.
description: JR Kagoshima-Chuo Station is the southern terminus of the Kyushu Shinkansen and our entry point to Kagoshima. The station is large and modern with shopping and dining. Ferries to Sakurajima depart nearby.

name: Shiroyama Observatory
summary: A hilltop viewpoint with Kagoshima's best views of Sakurajima volcano across the bay.
description: Shiroyama is a 107-metre hill in central Kagoshima, topped by an observatory with arguably the best views of Sakurajima from the city. The forested hill is also a nature reserve — keep an eye out for Yakushima macaques. The observatory is especially beautiful at sunset when Sakurajima is silhouetted against orange skies.

name: Sakurajima Ferry + Volcanic Trail
summary: A short ferry ride to an active volcano — hike through lava fields with views of ash-capped peaks.
description: Sakurajima is one of Japan's most active volcanoes, still puffing ash and smoke daily. The 15-minute ferry from Kagoshima port drops you on the volcanic island where you can walk through lava fields formed in the 1914 eruption, visit ash-buried torii gates, and admire the dramatic landscape. The ferry runs 24 hours and costs ¥160.

name: Sengan-en Garden
summary: A stunning 17th-century daimyo garden using Sakurajima as its borrowed landscape centrepiece.
description: Sengan-en was built in 1658 by the Shimadzu clan, Kyushu's most powerful feudal lords. The garden uses Sakurajima and Kagoshima Bay as its borrowed landscape — a masterpiece of the shakkei technique. The adjacent Shoko Shuseikan museum tells the story of Satsuma's industrial revolution. Allow a full morning here.

name: Tenmonkan Shopping Street
summary: Kagoshima's main covered shopping arcade — great for local specialities and souvenirs.
description: Tenmonkan is Kagoshima's bustling covered shopping arcade, a mix of fashion stores, restaurants, and souvenir shops. Look out for local specialities: Satsuma age (fried fish cakes), black pork products, and sweet shochu. The White Bear shaved ice (shirokuma) is a Kagoshima institution — try it here.

name: Kumasotei Restaurant
summary: Traditional Satsuma cuisine in a beautiful old townhouse — the best place for Kagoshima black pork.
description: Kumasotei is one of Kagoshima's most celebrated traditional restaurants, serving Satsuma-ryori (Satsuma cuisine) in a beautifully preserved Meiji-era townhouse. The speciality is Kagoshima kurobuta (black Berkshire pork) shabu-shabu or tonkatsu. Book ahead — it fills up fast.

name: Hoshino Resorts KAI Yufuin
summary: Luxury onsen ryokan nestled in the Yufuin valley — our base for one night.
description: KAI Yufuin is a Hoshino Resorts property offering a traditional ryokan experience with private onsen baths and kaiseki dining. Check-in is at 15:00 on 10 Dec and checkout at 12:00 on 11 Dec. Dinner (kaiseki) is at 17:30 on 10 Dec and breakfast is at 08:30 on 11 Dec — both included with the stay.
